proposed_description: Aggregate for non-accent Italian orthography: phonemic and sound-preserving spelling. Sibling axis of orthography.accent (AccentAuthor owns the accent MARK; this seat owns everything orthographic that is not the mark). Children are error-CLASS leaves. Every drill item is index-scored MCQ discrimination, so the apostrophe/accent fold in the grammar marker's norm() never bites (that fold is exactly why typed spelling is not deterministically gradable and MCQ is the correct instrument).

proposed_attributes.note: ID-FORM FLAG for Architecture: the accent sibling is orthography.accent.italian.* (carries a language segment); DISPATCH_spelling.md writes orthography.spelling.<class>, which I have followed here and in all 141 item markpoints. If you want strict parallelism, rename root -> orthography.spelling.italian and each leaf -> orthography.spelling.italian.<class>; it is a mechanical find-replace across this file + grammar_questions_orthography.json. Your call — I did not want to diverge from the dispatch unilaterally.

rationale: Gives the eight spelling error classes a home aggregate parallel to accent, so the coverage matrix and the drill's scope filter can address spelling as its own axis. The manifest topic 'orthography' already loads grammar_questions_orthography.json (created this session).

proposed_description: Phonemic geminate consonants written single (bellissimo -> bellisimo, cappello -> capello) OR a spurious double added where none belongs (dopo -> doppo, sabato -> sabbato). Italian's single biggest spelling-error source, and frequently meaning-bearing: cappello/capello (hat/hair), penna/pena (pen/pain), nonno/nono (grandfather/ninth), sette/sete (seven/thirst), cane/canne (dog/reeds), donna/dona.

proposed_attributes.registry: gemination.geminate_simplified (existing); gemination.spurious_double (PROPOSED — the add-a-double direction has no specific yet)

rationale: 30 items delivered. The doppie are phonemic, so this is a sound-distinction class (registry family 'gemination'), not merely visual. Minimal pairs make the strongest discrimination items.

proposed_description: The consonant digraphs/trigraph: gl(i) = /ly/ (famiglia, figlio), gn = /ny/ (bagno, sogno), sc + e/i = /sh/ (pesce, sciare, scienza). The error reduces the digraph to a plain letter (famiglia -> familia, bagno -> banio) or mis-selects it (pesce/pesche, where sce=/she/ vs sche=/ske/ separates fish from peaches).

proposed_attributes.common_miss: digraph spelled out with a plain letter + i
proposed_attributes.registry: orthography.digraph_reduced (PROPOSED — no digraph specific in the registry yet)

rationale: 16 items delivered. gl/gn have no English equivalent spelling, so L1-transfer errors (familia, banio) are systematic, not random.

proposed_description: Keeping the hard or soft value of c/g across inflection. Plurals: amica -> amiche (h keeps hard c) vs the soft exceptions amico -> amici, medico -> medici, biologo -> biologi; adjectives lungo -> lunghi / lunga -> lunghe. Verbs: -care/-gare insert h before e/i (cerchi, cercherò, paghi, paghiamo) while -ciare/-giare do NOT (mangiamo). Silent-i plurals cia/gia: drop after a consonant (arance) vs keep after a vowel (camicie). Lexical -cie- (cielo, scienza, efficiente).

proposed_attributes.canonical_example: amiche (h) vs amici (soft exception); cerchi (-care h)
proposed_attributes.registry: orthography.cg_plural_stress_misapplied, orthography.omitted_h_hard_cg, orthography.cia_gia_plural_i, orthography.double_i_retained (ALL existing) — this class is already well-served
proposed_attributes.note: Deliberate cross-tie: these forms are also produced under verb-formation and adjective/noun agreement. Per Rev 27 an item may carry a second markpoint on the owning grammar leaf where it genuinely evidences both; wave-1 items keep a single spelling markpoint (the drill's job is the spelling discrimination, index-scored).

rationale: 24 items delivered. The richest class linguistically and the best-covered in the existing registry; overlaps verb-formation (cerchi) and agreement (lunghe) intentionally.

proposed_description: The three ways Italian writes /kw/-ish sequences: default qu (quando, quale, cinque); cu in a closed lexical set (cuore, scuola, cui, innocuo); and the very small cqu family (acqua, acquisto, nacque, piacque). Errors: squola for scuola, quore for cuore (qu-overreach), aqua/acua for acqua (cqu reduced), and the over-correction aquila -> acquila.
rationale: 15 items delivered. cu and cqu are near-closed sets worth enumerating; qu is the productive default.

proposed_description: The silent, non-etymological h that distinguishes four present-tense forms of avere from ordinary words: ho/hai/ha/hanno vs o (or) / ai (to the) / a (to,at) / anno (year). Both directions: dropping the h (ho -> o) and adding it where it does not belong (anno -> hanno, a -> ha). Exclamative ah/oh sit here too.

proposed_attributes.canonical_example: ha (has) vs a (to); hanno (they have) vs anno (year)
proposed_attributes.common_miss: a for ha (dropped); hanno for anno (added/hypercorrection)
proposed_attributes.note: Registry family is accent_silent_letter (shared with AccentAuthor); the h is not an accent but the family already groups 'silent letters'. Boundary is clean: I own the h, AccentAuthor owns the accent mark.

rationale: 12 items delivered. A closed four-form set, each with a real homophone word, so every item is a genuine two-way discrimination.

proposed_description: The elision apostrophe: lo/la + vowel -> l' (l'acqua, l'uomo), una + vowel -> un' (un'amica) BUT masculine un takes NO apostrophe (un amico, un esame); di -> d' (d'accordo), preposition+article (all'una, dell'acqua), mezz'ora, senz'altro, Sant'Antonio, anch'io. Plus the truncation po' (poco) and the no-apostrophe qual è (qual'e is the error). The gender split (un' feminine vs un masculine) is the highest-value contrast.

proposed_attributes.canonical_example: un'amica (f) vs un amico (m); qual è (no apostrophe)
proposed_attributes.common_miss: un'amico (apostrophe on masculine); una amica (unelided); qual'è (spurious)
proposed_attributes.registry: orthography.failed_elision (existing — scope currently 'clitic not elided'; PROPOSE widening to cover un-elided articles); orthography.elision_gender_error, orthography.spurious_apostrophe (both PROPOSED)
proposed_attributes.boundary: po'/va' truncation apostrophes are ALSO claimed by AccentAuthor's proposed orthography.accent.italian.placement.apostrophe_not_accent. Smith 2026-07-21: 'author away for apostrophes ... doesn't matter who makes the qs' — so I have authored the elision set (incl. one po' item) here; Architecture to decide final placement / de-dupe. No double coverage intended.

rationale: 24 items delivered. Smith's seed class ('un amica / un'amica / una amica'). The un'/un gender rule is the sharpest single teaching point and is undrillable typed (norm() folds the apostrophe), so MCQ is the only correct instrument.

proposed_description: Italian lower-cases what English capitalises: weekdays (lunedì), months (gennaio), languages and nationalities whether adjective or noun (italiano, gli italiani, l'inglese), and courtesy titles (signor Rossi). The error is English-transfer over-capitalisation mid-sentence.

proposed_attributes.canonical_example: lunedì, gennaio, italiano (all lower case mid-sentence)
proposed_attributes.note: Items are whole-sentence choices differing only in one word's case, and the target word is placed MID-sentence so lower case is unambiguously correct (sentence-initial capitals are legitimate).

rationale: 12 items delivered. Pure L1-transfer error; a recognition instrument (whole-sentence MCQ) fits it exactly.

proposed_description: Lexical confusion of unstressed vowels: i/e (difficile not difficele, dicembre not decembre, minuto not menuto) and o/u (comprare not cumprare, novembre not nuvembre). Minor and item-specific rather than rule-governed; lower priority than the phonemic classes above.

proposed_attributes.common_miss: unstressed i heard/written as e; o as u
proposed_attributes.registry: orthography.vowel_confusion (PROPOSED — the existing io_plural_i_count and i_retention_error are narrower morphological cases, not this lexical one)

rationale: 8 items delivered (kept small, per the dispatch's 'minor, lexical' note). Included so the class has a coverage cell rather than falling into an aggregate.
